description: The presented image is an unrelated "Check for updates" placeholder icon, indicating that the target material science figure associated with the study of Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel failed to load correctly. No valid information related to the steel's microstructure, mechanical properties, characterization results, processing parameters or experimental data can be extracted from this invalid placeholder, so all content of the intended figure for this study is completely unreadable.

This figure characterizes the microstructure, phase constitution and cryogenic phase stability of Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel. Subfigure (a) is the EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) map with a 50 μm scale bar, showing equiaxed austenite grains with diverse crystallographic orientations corresponding to the IPF color triangle marking <001>, <101> and <111> orientations. Subfigure (b) is the EBSD phase map with a 50 μm scale bar, confirming the initial steel consists of single austenite phase (all marked in red). Subfigure (c) presents XRD patterns of initial sample and sample held at -196 °C for 30 min, both only show characteristic γ austenite peaks at γ(111), γ(200), γ(220) and γ(311) without martensite peaks, verifying the extremely high stability of austenite that no isothermal martensitic transformation occurs at -196 °C.

This figure presents tensile test results of Fe–20Mn–6Al–0.6C–0.15Si austenitic low-density steel tested at 25 °C (room temperature, RT), -77 °C, and -196 °C. Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves: as test temperature decreases, both peak engineering stress (ultimate tensile strength, UTS) and total elongation increase, with UTS reaching ~1280 MPa and total elongation ~67% at -196 °C. Subplot (b) displays true stress-strain curves and work hardening rate curves: work hardening rate drops sharply at the initial deformation stage; it stays nearly linear at -77 °C and -196 °C in the subsequent deformation stage, while decreases continuously at RT, and higher work hardening rate is observed at lower temperature. The intersection of true stress and work hardening rate corresponds to the necking point, which occurs at higher true strain under lower temperature. Subplot (c) illustrates temperature dependence of properties: total elongation (TE), uniform elongation (UE), yield strength (YS) and UTS all increase with decreasing temperature, with fitted linear relationships YS=474.6-2.05*T and UTS=813.0-2.36*T (T in °C). The results confirm the studied steel achieves simultaneous strength and ductility improvement at cryogenic temperatures, supporting its cryogenic application potential.

This figure presents EBSD characterization results of tensile-deformed Fe–20Mn–6Al–0.6C austenitic low-density steel tested at three temperatures: -196 °C (a,d,g), -77 °C (b,e,h), and room temperature (RT, c,f,i). Subfigures a-c are inverse pole figure (IPF) maps, where mechanical twins formed at -196 °C are marked by arrows. Subfigures d-f are grain boundary (GB) maps: red lines represent low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs, 2-15°), blue lines represent high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs, >15°), with LAGB fractions of 70.2% at -196 °C, 63.0% at -77 °C, and 56.0% at RT. Subfigures g-i are higher-magnification kernel average misorientation maps for geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) distribution, overlaid with GB maps of the framed regions in d-f. GNDs are observed to accumulate at LAGBs, and LAGB fraction increases with decreasing deformation temperature, supporting that lower temperature reduces stacking fault energy and activates additional deformation mechanisms including mechanical twinning at cryogenic conditions.

This figure presents TEM characterization results of Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel after tensile deformation at different cryogenic temperatures. Subfigure (a) shows the bright-field TEM image of the sample deformed at -196 °C, with its corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (zone axis Z.A. [110]) inset. Dislocations and mechanical twins formed on {111} plane along <112> direction are clearly observed in (a), confirmed by the split diffraction spots in SAED. Subfigure (b) shows the TEM image of the sample deformed at -77 °C, where dense microbands aligned along traces of {111} slip planes and high-density dislocations are found, with no mechanical twins detected. The observation reveals that decreasing deformation temperature and corresponding stacking fault energy change the dominant deformation mechanism from planar glide forming microbands at -77 °C to mechanical twinning at -196 °C. Both microstructures act as barriers for dislocation motion, contributing to the high work-hardening rate and excellent low-temperature mechanical properties of the steel.

This figure presents Charpy impact performance of the Fe–20Mn–6Al–0.6C–0.15Si austenitic low-density steel tested at -196 °C, -77 °C, and room temperature (RT, 25 °C). Subplot (a) shows Charpy impact load versus deflection curves: the peak impact load increases while the fracture deflection decreases as temperature drops. The peak load reaches ~32 kN at -196 °C with fracture deflection ~9.5 mm, while the RT sample has a peak load of ~24 kN and fracture deflection over 25 mm. Subplot (b) is a stacked bar chart of impact absorbed energy, divided into crack initiation energy (W_initiation), stable crack propagation energy (W_stable), and unstable crack propagation energy (W_unstable). Total absorbed energy is 188 J at -196 °C, 277 J at -77 °C, and 327 J at RT, showing a decreasing trend with lower temperature. The crack initiation energy increases with decreasing temperature, while the proportion of crack propagation energy reduces. The results confirm that the studied steel retains favorable impact toughness at cryogenic temperatures, with its temperature-dependent impact performance correlated to variations in deformation mechanisms and crack growth resistance at different temperatures.

This figure presents fracture surfaces of Charpy V-notched impact samples of Fe-20Mn-6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel tested at three different temperatures: (a, d) -196 °C, (b, e) -77 °C, (c, f) room temperature. Subfigures a, b, c are macroscopic fracture morphologies with 1 mm scale bar, marked with V-notch, fibrous zone (for -77 °C and room temperature samples), radial zone and shear lip, showing typical ductile fracture macro-characteristics at all test temperatures. Subfigures d, e, f are high-magnification SEM images, with scale bars of 100 μm (d) and 20 μm (e, f). Dimples are observed in all high-magnification images: the -196 °C sample has mixed large dimples, small dimples and tear edges, while dimples become more uniform and larger as temperature rises to room temperature. The results confirm the steel maintains ductile fracture mode from room temperature to -196 °C, verifying its excellent cryogenic toughness.

This plot illustrates the dependence of tensile elongation of austenitic steels (the studied Fe-20Mn-6Al-0.6C low-density austenitic steel in this work) on tensile test temperature and stacking fault energy (SFE). The x-axis increases with higher test temperature/SFE, while glide planarity enhances towards the left direction (lower temperature/SFE). The plot is divided into three regions: Region I (high temperature/SFE) corresponds to wavy glide with low tensile elongation; Region II (medium temperature/SFE) corresponds to planar glide without deformation-induced martensitic transformation (DIMT), where tensile elongation rises to the peak value; Region III (low temperature/SFE) corresponds to DIMT with the highest glide planarity, where tensile elongation slightly decreases from the peak but remains higher than that in Region I. The curve demonstrates that evolution of deformation mechanisms with temperature/SFE dominates the tensile plasticity variation of austenitic steels, which explains the excellent low-temperature ductility of the studied Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel.

This plot displays lnσ as a function of lnε for Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel tested at three different temperatures to derive work hardening exponents, with -196 °C marked by blue triangles, -77 °C marked by red circles, and room temperature (RT) marked by grey squares. Each curve presents two separate work hardening stages with distinct n values: n₁=0.204, n₂=0.448 for -196 °C; n₁=0.181, n₂=0.439 for -77 °C; n₁=0.174, n₂=0.362 for RT. Both n values at each stage rise with decreasing test temperature, demonstrating that lower temperature brings improved work hardening capacity, which supports the superior tensile ductility of the steel under cryogenic conditions.

This figure presents Charpy impact mechanical test results of Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel. Subplots (a), (b), (c) show smoothed impact load-deflection curves at -196 °C, -77 °C, and room temperature respectively, with both experimental curves and ISO-evaluation curves plotted. Each curve is divided into four absorbed energy regions: W_initiation (crack initiation), W_stable (stable crack growth), W_unstable (unstable crack growth), and W_collapse (final collapse). Characteristic loads including general yield load Fgy, crack initiation load Fin, maximum impact load Fm, unstable crack initiation load Fiu, and unstable crack termination load Fa are marked. Rebound compliance (RC, reflecting unstable crack growth rate) is 10.9957 at -196 °C, 4.6215 at -77 °C, and 3.7864 at room temperature, increasing with decreasing temperature. Subplot (d) shows temperature dependence of characteristic values from -200 °C to 50 °C: Fgy, Fm, Fin all increase as temperature decreases, and Fiu/Fm ratio also rises at lower temperatures. The figure supports that the studied steel has higher crack initiation resistance at cryogenic temperatures, but faster unstable crack propagation and lower crack propagation energy lead to reduced overall impact toughness at lower temperatures.

This figure presents Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) analysis results of the near-fracture microstructure of Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel after impact tests. Subfigure (a) shows the grain orientation map of the sample tested at -196 °C, subfigure (b) shows the grain orientation map of the room temperature (RT) tested sample, both with a 200 μm scale bar and an inverse pole figure color legend for crystal orientation indexing. Subfigure (c) displays the misorientation profile along the line crossing the crack in marked Grain A, with x-axis as distance in μm and y-axis as misorientation angle in °. Crack propagation paths are zigzag for both test temperatures, showing transgranular fracture features; secondary cracks are observed in the RT sample but not in the -196 °C sample. The misorientation across the crack is less than 15°, verifying transgranular crack propagation, while the orientation gradient within Grain A indicates severe plastic deformation near the crack. The secondary cracks in RT sample release stress concentration, delaying fracture and contributing to higher impact toughness at RT.

This group of three SEM micrographs presents the near-fracture microstructures of Fe-20Mn-6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel after Charpy impact tests at different temperatures: subfigure (a) corresponds to -196 °C, (b) to -77 °C, and (c) to room temperature, with a 20 μm scale bar for each subfigure. Yellow arrows mark the planar glide features in the deformed austenite matrix. Only sparse planar glide regions are observed in the sample tested at -196 °C, while the density of planar glide features increases remarkably as test temperature rises. More pronounced planar glide at higher temperatures retards crack propagation to improve impact toughness, while limited activated planar glide at cryogenic temperatures under high impact strain rate leads to lower crack propagation resistance, explaining the reduced cryogenic impact toughness of the investigated steel.
